Curriculum → High-level design interview prep → Foundations and estimation → Availability, consistency, and CAP trade-offs

Lesson 5 of 5

Availability, consistency, and CAP trade-offs

Designs under partition must choose whether to prioritize fresh coordinated answers or continued service with potentially stale data.

25-35 min HLD flow Foundations and estimation 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

Many interview prompts become easier when you can place each operation on the spectrum between strict correctness and graceful degradation.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Use precise definitions Durability is separate from both availability and consistency2. Read CAP correctly Account settings may favor consistency over availability3. Explain the user impact Name the degraded mode you will accept during partition

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing Many interview prompts become easier when you can place each operation on the spectrum between strict correctness and graceful degradation. 2. Use precise definitions Durability is separate from both availability and consistency3. Read CAP correctly Account settings may favor consistency over availability4. Explain the user impact Name the degraded mode you will accept during partition
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

Many interview prompts become easier when you can place each operation on the spectrum between strict correctness and graceful degradation.

What to say first

  • State the consistency requirement per critical operation.
  • Describe the system behavior during partition, failover, and recovery.
  • Differentiate strong, eventual, and session-level guarantees.
2

Study section

Use precise definitions

Availability means every request receives a non-error response. Consistency means readers observe the most recent successful write according to the chosen model.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Durability is separate from both availability and consistency
  • Different operations in the same product may need different consistency levels
  • Partitions are not rare edge cases in distributed systems
3

Study section

Read CAP correctly

During a network partition, a distributed system cannot fully provide both availability and strong consistency for every operation. Real systems make operation-specific choices.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Account settings may favor consistency over availability
  • Feed ranking may prefer availability with eventual consistency
  • Quorums, leaders, and leases help you navigate the trade-off space
4

Study section

Explain the user impact

Trade-offs are easier to justify when you connect them to visible outcomes: duplicate notifications, stale counters, temporary read-only modes, or retriable write failures.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Name the degraded mode you will accept during partition
  • Mention reconciliation or repair mechanisms
  • Show where product semantics allow stale or approximate answers

Reference diagram

Visual anchor

A useful mental model for explaining partition-time trade-offs.

Adapted from The System Design Primer by Donne Martin (CC BY 4.0).

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • State the consistency requirement per critical operation.
  • Describe the system behavior during partition, failover, and recovery.
  • Differentiate strong, eventual, and session-level guarantees.
  • Explain the user-visible consequence of each trade-off.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Saying a whole product is simply CP or AP without qualifying the operation.
  • Equating eventual consistency with random inconsistency.
  • Ignoring repair work after the partition heals.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • Which parts of a social feed can be eventually consistent?
  • How would you design an order placement flow during a regional partition?
  • When is temporary unavailability preferable to stale reads?

Related topics

Keep the thread going

Replication and failover Data storage and indexingIdempotency, retries, and back pressure Caching, asynchrony, and resilience

Simulation lab

Simulation lab: Availability, consistency, and CAP trade-offs

Turn estimation and baseline trade-offs into an actual workload so you can see which assumption breaks first. Apply it to availability, consistency, and cap trade-offs so the trade-offs feel concrete.

PRACTICE GUIDE

Explain the concept Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Explain the concept

Teach "Availability, consistency, and CAP trade-offs" back to an interviewer. Summarize what it is, when you would use it, and why it matters in a real system.

Goal

Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Guardrails

  • Which parts of a social feed can be eventually consistent?
  • Use precise definitions
  • Read CAP correctly
  • State the consistency requirement per critical operation.

Suggested Structure

  • Definition
  • When to use it
  • Benefits
  • Trade-offs

Bring your own model
